issue_title: `toml.loads` spins forever parsing a table entry with whitespace and dots
issue_body:
On 0.9.4 (and current master), adding a table entry value with (unnecessary) padding whitespace and dots (which looks like the spec allows?) seems to end up in an infinite loop:

```
Python 3.6.4 (default, Dec 19 2017, 14:48:12) 
[GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)] on linux
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import toml
>>> toml.__version__
'0.9.4'
>>> toml.loads('[ "white.space" ]')
```

At that point, it spins forever (or until I ctrl-c it).

It does just seem to be the combination of the two; either on their own is fine:

```
>>> toml.loads('["no.white.space"]')
{'no.white.space': {}}
>>> toml.loads('[ "nodot" ]')
{'nodot': {}}
```

issue_title: Add sendfile() support
issue_body:
Hello David,
would you be interested in a patch adding sendfile() support?
To read more about sendfile you can take a look at my [python wrapper](https://github.com/giampaolo/pysendfile) which I successfully used it [pyftpdlib]( https://github.com/giampaolo/pyftpdlib).
sendfile is available as [os.sendfile()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/os.html#os.sendfile) starting from python 3.3.

Some considerations:

- sendfile can be used with non-blocking sockets, meaning that in case the socket is not "write ready" you immediately get EAGAIN
- with non blocking sockets sendfile() automatically returns "sooner", sending (and returning) fewer bytes than requested
- on the other hand, the internal kernel-space operation moving the buffer from the regular file fd to the socket fd can block, similarly to plain `file.read()`

As such I'm not sure what's best to do. Having used sendfile() for years in pyftpdlib my impression is that disk IO is usually fast enough to not represent an actual problem for the main IO loop.
On the other hand I see that curio explicitly promotes the usage of a thread pool when it comes to dealing with files (`aopen()` etc.).  So here's a possible proposal:

- add a `Socket.sendfile(fd, maxsize=65536)` method which by default tries to send the file using sendfile()
- if sendfile() fails on the first call (because fd is not a regular fd), internally we fallback on using the plain `chunk = file.read(maxsize) -> sock.sendall(chunk)` methodology. This mimics the semantic of [socket.sendfile()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/socket.html#socket.socket.sendfile) which I contributed some time ago and which is currently not exposed in the `Socket` class.
- by default `Socket.sendfile` *does not* use a thread pool, but if the user is worried about blocking he/she can optionally do so with `await run_in_thread(s.sendfile, fd)`. 
 
Thoughts?

issue_title: Summary ticket for ``hu_HU`` provider
issue_body:
Summary ticket for issues with the ``hu_HU`` provider (I'm working on a PR to fix these).

- [ ] ``providers.address.street_name`` (and descendants): incorrectly capitalises street/place/etc.
- [ ] ``providers.address.street_address_with_county``: does not capitalise place name
- [ ] ``providers.automotive.license_plate``: seems to be the UK licence plate structure, the correct Hungarian licence plate regex is ``\d{3}-[A-Z]{3}``
- [ ] ``providers.color.color_name``: currently providers results in English
- [ ] ``provides.company.company_suffix``: misspellings and miscapitalizations (e.g. ``Zrt.`` as ``ZRT``
- [ ] ``providers.job``: this could be replaced with a professions list from KSH
- [ ] ``providers.phone_number``: add specific providers that generate only mobile (``20``, ``30``, ``60``,  ``70``) numbers, Budapest numbers (``1``) and landline numbers
- [ ] ``providers.ssn``: this seems to generate a 'személyi szám'. Other important IDs are the társadalombiztosítási azonosító jel (TAJ szám, format ``\d{3} \d{3} \d{3}``), which is more akin to an SSN in its functionality, and the personal ID card number (``\d{6}[A-Z]{2}``)

issue_title: dpkt.http.Response: server cookies not handled correctly
issue_body:
_From [simdr...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/116904784437902388373/) on March 29, 2009 18:39:43_

What steps will reproduce the problem? data = """HTTP/1.x 200 OK\r\nSet-Cookie: first_cookie=cookie1; path=/;
domain=.example.com\r\nSet-Cookie: second_cookie=cookie2; path=/;
domain=.example.com\r\nContent-Length: 0\r\n\r\n"""
http = dpkt.http.Response(data)
print http What is the expected output? What do you see instead? Only the last server cookie "survives" [example script attached]

Original:
\----------------------------------------
HTTP/1.x 200 OK
Set-Cookie: first_cookie=cookie1; path=/; domain=.example.com
Set-Cookie: second_cookie=cookie2; path=/; domain=.example.com
Content-Length: 0
\----------------------------------------

Parsed:
\----------------------------------------
HTTP/1.x 200 OK
set-cookie: second_cookie=cookie2; path=/; domain=.example.com
content-length: 0
---------------------------------------- What version of the product are you using? On what operating system? dpkt 1.6 (Ubuntu + Windows XP), python 2.5.4 Please provide any additional information below. The problem is in dpkt.http.parse_headers() - it doesn't take into account
multiple headers with the same name and thus overwrites the value of
"set-cookie" with the value of the last "Set-Cookie" header.

Multiple cookies are very common in real world traffic (i.e. google.com,
facebook.com)
Fixing this bug would really improve this excellent module (thanks dug!)

**Attachment:** [dpkt_cookies.py](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=18)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=18_

issue_title: Error in serialcommunicator thread when using other threads in application
issue_body:
The serial communicator can cause a lockup.

If there is no data then the run() thread in serialcommunicator can never yield causing the other threads in the user application to never run.

Solution:
Add a time.sleep(0) to the end of the run loop

  File "/home/me/Scripts/kotnetcli/build/build/kotnetcli/out00-PYZ.pyz/keyring.core", line 158, in <module>
  File "/home/me/Scripts/kotnetcli/build/build/kotnetcli/out00-PYZ.pyz/keyring.core", line 58, in init_backend
  File "/home/me/Scripts/kotnetcli/build/build/kotnetcli/out00-PYZ.pyz/keyring.core", line 66, in _get_best_keyring
  File "/home/me/Scripts/kotnetcli/build/build/kotnetcli/out00-PYZ.pyz/keyring.util", line 24, in wrapper
  File "/home/me/Scripts/kotnetcli/build/build/kotnetcli/out00-PYZ.pyz/keyring.backend", line 137, in get_all_keyring

ImportError: No module named file
[me@pc build]$ 
```

Solution:

First, edit the .spec file: the hiddenimport should look like this:

```
hiddenimports = ['keyring.backends.file',
                 'keyring.backends.Gnome',
                 'keyring.backends.Google',
                 'keyring.backends.keyczar',
                 'keyring.backends.kwallet',
                 'keyring.backends.multi',
                 'keyring.backends.OS_X',
                 'keyring.backends.pyfs',
                 'keyring.backends.SecretService',
                 'keyring.backends.Windows']
```

If you compile right now, the compiled binary will use `keyring.backends.file` as the keyring manager. You might want to use the Gnome keyring manager, like I did. But even when you force the keyring to do so (using `keyring.set_keyring(keyring.backends.Gnome.Keyring())`), it will not work, because there's something wrong with `Gnome.py`. To solve this problem: edit and put this at the top of `/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/keyring/backends/Gnome.py` :

    import datetime
    from gi.repository import GnomeKeyring

Save and try to compile again. It should work now.

issue_title: [folia] Let xpath() fail more informatively
issue_body: Right now, the XML tree is discarded unless `mode=XPATH` was a keyword argument. If the `xpath` function is called on a document not in this mode, it fails with `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'xpath'`, which can be confusing. It's probably easy and good practice to check the mode in that method before trying to make the call that can lead to that error, and raise a more informative exception when appropriate.

issue_title: Fatal error: 'GitRepoRunner' object has no attribute 'target'
issue_body:
I have been using `git-repo` for a while and I noticed that quite often it fails to clone a repository.
It outputs:
```
Fatal error: 'GitRepoRunner' object has no attribute 'target'
```
The thing is that if you try running command again - it may fail again. Or work. This was totally random, but luckily I managed to figure it out.

To start with, `git-repo` uses `KeywordArgumentParser` to parse command line params. It iterates over all args using `for arg, value in self.args.items()`. However, we need to remember that `self.arg.items()` is not ordered. So we process args in random order. 
Because of that, sometimes we process `<namespace>/<repo>` parameter before `<target>` parameter, sometimes after. And in cases when we process `<namespace>/<repo>` before `<target>` we have the issue.
`<namespace>/<repo>` arg is proccessed by `set_repo_slug` in `repo.py`. This function uses `self.target` value: `service = RepositoryService.get_service(None, self.target)`. And that's the problem. If the `<target>` was not parsed before `<namespace>/<repo>` we get an error here.

So there are two possible solutions:
1. Change `set_repo_slug` so it doesn't use `self.target` anywhere.
2. Ensure somehow that `<target>` is always processed before `<namespace>/<repo>`

issue_title: in producer, max_block_ms should larger than request_timeout_ms by default
issue_body:
Suppose we have a kafka producer initialized with default arguments, 60s as max_block_ms and 30s as request_timeout_ms. Then we run a loop:
```python
while True:
    producer.send(topic, value)
```

After we kill all processes in kafka-cluster, I want the producer raise an exception `KafkaTimeoutError`.

but when **max_block_ms > request_timeout_ms**, the exception will never be raised out.

Only when **max_block_ms < request_timeout_ms**, Exception will raised:
> KafkaTimeoutError: Failed to allocate memory within the configured max blocking time

My analyse:

when kafka producer try to send, it will block at most request_timeout_ms, after that it will drop those messages and clean send buffer. So, the producer can put new messages into the send buffer. The result is we can't receive any notice but our messages are droped exactly.

So, I think request_timeout_ms should never larger than max_block_ms. What do you think?

issue_title: in producer, max_block_ms should larger than request_timeout_ms by default
issue_body:
Suppose we have a kafka producer initialized with default arguments, 60s as max_block_ms and 30s as request_timeout_ms. Then we run a loop:
```python
while True:
    producer.send(topic, value)
```

After we kill all processes in kafka-cluster, I want the producer raise an exception `KafkaTimeoutError`.

but when max_block_ms > request_timeout_ms, the exception will never be raised out.

Only when max_block_ms < request_timeout_ms, Exception will raised:
> KafkaTimeoutError: Failed to allocate memory within the configured max blocking time

So I think when kafka producer try to send, it will block at most request_timeout_ms, after that it will drop those messages and clean send buffer. So, the producer can put new messages into the send buffer. The result is we can't receive any notice but our messages are droped exactly.

So, I think request_timeout_ms should never larger than max_block_ms. What do you think?

issue_title: add json property to HttpResponse
chardet 3.0 seems to have broken something
issue_body:
In pdfquery.py, in `smart_unicode_decode` is this:

    # detect encoding
    detected_encoding = chardet.detect(encoded_string)

With chardet 2.3.0, `detected_encoding` is `{'confidence': 0.0, 'encoding': None}`

With chardet 3.0.1 (newest as of time of writing this), `detected_encoding` is `None`

So it's crashing on the next line, where it does `detected_encoding['encoding']`.

Presumably, the fix is as simply as changing:

`encoding=detected_encoding['encoding'] or 'utf8',`

to

`encoding=detected_encoding['encoding'] if (detected_encoding and detected_encoding['encoding']) else 'utf8',

issue_title: Remove deprecated scripts like mlogversion, mlogdistinct, ...
let easyopen explicitly set the IDD file
issue_body:
**Problem:** idf=easyopen(fname) does not work if E+ is not installed

**Solution:** pass idd file as an arg to easyopen. So it becomes idf=easyopen(fname, idd=None) OR idf=easyopen(fname, iddname). When E+ is not installed idf=easyopen(fname, iddname) will work

issue_title: TrendTwitter

As in the title.

Key changes in upstream CEF between commits a6c42a7...d7f087e (since CEF Python 55.1):
* Update to Chromium version 55.0.2883.59 (Chrome beta channel from Nov 30)
* Fix crash when persisting user preferences
* Fix crash related to channel ID support, disable channel ID support
* Fix load error when using Request->SetUrl when url contains double byte characters
* Net security (CT, HSTS) expiration based on build age is now disabled by default. Configurable with CefSettings.enable_net_security_expiration.
* Add CefSettings.enable_net_security_expiration
* Standardize IME callbacks for off-screen rendering. Add CefRenderHandler.OnImeCompositionRangeChanged. Add CefBrowser IME methods : ImeSetComposition, ImeCommitText, ImeFinishComposingText, ImeCancelComposition
* Add CefWaitableEvent for synchronizing threads (one waits for another to finish work)
* Add CefThread for creating new CEF threads
* Add initial WebUI support. Visit chrome://webui-hosts for the list of supported hosts:
  - chrome://accessibility
  - chrome://appcache-internals

issue_title: Question: best way to use multiple indicators
issue_body:
Hi,

I would like to know your opinion about if this way is the best one to develop these rules:
```
Stoch = Stochastic(10,3,3)
Last = Last Price
PivotPoints(S2, S1, P, R1, R2)
Opt1 = 10

OpenLong  = Stoch >Opt1                 AND Last crosses below S1
OpenShort = Stoch <(100-Opt1)         AND Last crosses above R1

CloseLong  = Last crosses above P
CloseShort = Last crosses below P
```

CODE:
```
self.stoch=indicator.Stochastic(self.data1,period=period,period_dfast=period_dfast,period_dslow=period_dslow)
self.constantValue=10
self.constantValue2=100-self.constantValue
 self.pp = indicator.PivotPoint(self.data1)
self.s1=self.pp.lines.s1
self.s2=self.pp.lines.s2
self.r1=self.pp.lines.r1
self.r2=self.pp.lines.r2
self.signal = indicator.CrossDown(self.data1.close, self.s1)
self.signal2=indicator.Cmp(self.stoch,self.value)
self.signal3=indicator.And(self.signal,self.signal2)
self.signal4=indicator.Cmp(self.stoch,self.value2)
self.signal5 = indicator.CrossOver(self.data1.close, self.r1)
self.signal6 = indicator.CrossOver(self.data1.close, self.pp)
self.signal7 = indicator.CrossDown(self.data1.close, self.pp)
self.signal8=indicator.And(self.signal5,self.signal4)
```
Rgds,
JJ

issue_title: Installing _thread makes other modules believe they run python 3
issue_body:
Installing `pies2overrides` installs a `_thread.py` in the site-packages directory causing errors like this:
```
File "env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nexus/modules.py", line 46, in __getattribute__
   NexusModule.set_global('site', object.__getattribute__(self, 'site'))
 File "env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/nexus/modules.py", line 51, in set_global
   ident = thread.get_ident()
AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'get_ident'
```
This happens because some modules (like nexus) try to import `_thread` if it exists and otherwise fall back to `thread`:
```
# import thread lib from py3k or 2
try:
    import _thread as thread
except ImportError:
    import thread
```

issue_title: Pointer does not move with ecodes.EV_REL
issue_body:
I am trying to move the mouse visually on screen for an automation tool that I am working on, it seems the mouse pointer does not move with ecodes.EV_REL. I am working on Ubuntu and I tried on both X11 and Mir display server. python-uinput seems to be working fine though, but our project heavily uses python-evdev so we need to keep using it. Below is the code that reproduces the issue.

```
from time import sleep

from evdev import UInput, ecodes

ui = UInput()
for i in range(20):
    ui.write(ecodes.EV_REL, ecodes.REL_X, 5)
    ui.write(ecodes.EV_REL, ecodes.REL_Y, 5)
    ui.syn()
    sleep(0.01)
```

That codes does run without errors but it does not do anything, OTH, mouse click events do work.

issue_title: partition not owned by database owner
issue_body:
I have a database owned by django:

```mobilesurvey=# \l
                                     List of databases
       Name       |  Owner   | Encoding |   Collate   |    Ctype    |   Access privileges   
------------------+----------+----------+-------------+-------------+-----------------------
 mobilesurvey     | django   | UTF8     | en_US.utf8  | en_US.utf8  | 
```

... and my app connects to the database with the django database user, but the partitions are being created for the postgres user:

```
 Schema |            Name            | Type  |  Owner   
--------+----------------------------+-------+----------
 ...
 public | rm_signup_survey_19_19     | table | postgres
 public | rm_signup_survey_23_23     | table | postgres
 ...
```

My app was having problems inserting records, so I tried it by hand, using the django user, and I received:
ERROR:  permission denied for relation rm_signup_survey_19_19

I have another server where this is working correctly (inserts succeeding), and the partition tables are being created as the django user there:

```
 Schema |            Name            | Type  |  Owner   
--------+----------------------------+-------+----------
 ...
 public | rm_signup_survey_5_5       | table | django
 public | rm_signup_survey_6_6       | table | django
 ...
```

... and I assume that's the problem. 

The database owner is still django, but the permissions do look different:

```
     Name     |  Owner   | Encoding |   Collate   |    Ctype    |   Access privileges   
--------------+----------+----------+-------------+-------------+-----------------------
 mobilesurvey | django   | UTF8     | en_US.utf8  | en_US.utf8  | =Tc/django           +
              |          |          |             |             | django=CTc/django    +
              |          |          |             |             | reader=c/django
```


How can I fix this?

issue_title: NamedTuples do not validate as tuples or named tuples
issue_body:
```
from collections import namedtuple

NT = namedtuple('NT', ['a', 'b'])

nt = NT(1, 2)
t = (1, 2)

Schema((int, int))(nt)    # => Invalid
Schema((int, int))(t)     # => Valid
Schema(NT(int, int))(nt)  # => Invalid
Schema(NT(int, int))(t)   # => Valid
````

Given that `NT(1, 2) == (1, 2) => True` I'd expect all of the above cases to return as valid.

issue_title: adjustments for bonds in regexp required
issue_body:
happy finding your lib.
some segments are different if holdings do contain bonds, I added below to retrieve  price and pieces which would be otherwise blank.

example
:70E::HOLD//1NZD266,937
93B::AGGR//FAMT/2000,00

```
class MT535_Miniparser:
    re_identification = re.compile(r"^:35B:ISIN\s(.*)\|(.*)\|(.*)$")
    re_marketprice = re.compile(r"^:90B::MRKT\/\/ACTU\/([A-Z]{3})(\d*),{1}(\d*)$")
    re_marketprice_b = re.compile(r"^:90A::MRKT\/\/PRCT\/(\d*),{1}(\d*)$")
    re_pricedate = re.compile(r"^:98A::PRIC\/\/(\d*)$")
    re_pieces = re.compile(r"^:93B::AGGR\/\/UNIT\/(\d*),(\d*)$")
    re_pieces_b = re.compile(r"^:93B::AGGR\/\/FAMT\/(\d*),(\d*)$")
    re_totalvalue = re.compile(r"^:19A::HOLD\/\/([A-Z]{3})(\d*),{1}(\d*)$")
    re_acquisitionprice = re.compile(r"^:70E::HOLD\/\/\d*STK\|2(\d*?),{1}(\d*?)\+([A-Z]{3})$")

    #re_acquisitionprice_b = re.compile(r"^:70E::HOLD\/\/\d*([A-Z])\|2(\d*?),{1}(\d*?)$")
    re_acquisitionprice_b = re.compile(r"^:70E::HOLD\/\/\d*([A-Z]{3})\|2(\d*?),{1}(\d*?)$")
```

issue_title: switch to OneShotObserverList for the API
issue_body:
At present, the `verify()` API can only be called once, because it only stashes a single Deferred. It needs a Deferred because it might be called before or after the verifier becomes available).

It'd be nice to relax this requirement. To do so, I think I'd use a `OneShotObserverList` (like the one I implemented in Tahoe, but without the `eventual()` call that makes it harder to test synchronously).

I've just installed powerline-shell and have been working through the readme but have been left a bit confused at the config section. 

> Config File
> Powerline-shell is customizable through the use of a config file. This file is expected to be located at ~/.config/powerline-shell/config.json. You can generate the default config at this location using:
>
> powerline-shell --generate-config > ~/.powerline-shell.json
> (As an example, my config file is located here: here)

You state the config file is expected to be in **~/.config/powerline-shell/config.json** but then give the command to generate it under **~/** with a completely different name. So, which is it? The first stated location and filename, or the second?

Also you provide a link to your own config as an example. The link provided ends on a 404 not found.

Thanks for your time and hope you can provide some clarity.

issue_title: Incorrect logic for symlinks
issue_body:
Please check commit https://github.com/goldmann/docker-squash/commit/afd81aefb5230ef8f001fec544e8649984427444

It is not correct, because you are checking for symlinks' targets in the list `squashed_files` (`member.linkname not in squashed_files`) which CAN BE NOT FILLED YET FOR TARGET FILES. This is incorrect.

You need to add symlinks to a temporary list and iterate through them after processing ALL other type of members in `layer_tar`, this will avoid the critical bug you created in the commit :)

Do you need a help in resolving the issue?

issue_title: Discrepancy between check-manifest and sdist
issue_body:
There appears to be a discrepancy between what `check-manifest --ignore "depends/*"` complains is missing, and what `python setup.py sdist --dry-run --verbose` actually includes.

For example, the former complains Tests/*.py are missing, but the latter includes them.

Is this a bug in check-manifest, or am I missing something?

Repo to repro:
```bash
git clone -b manifest https://git@github.com/wiredfool/Pillow.git
cd Pillow
```

check-manifest==0.30

include *.md

issue_title: get_as_df start parameter seems to not working
issue_body:
I am trying to import data in pandas.dataframe format using pygsheets API. 
My sheet looks like: 

> 

Store code | Lenght of interview
-- | --
Sto_code | leng_int
3082 | 34
1718 | 18
1434 | 24
739 | 18
6378 | 38
1365 | 20
1201 | 18
6406 | 23
3365 | 25

So I would like to use the second row as a header. To do it I am calling the **get_as_pd** method:
```
 def get_content_as_pd_dataframe(self, worksheet, start="A2"):
        # by default we skip the first row
        logger.info("Get dataframe from worksheet " +str(worksheet) + " strart from cell " + str(start))
        return worksheet.get_as_df(start=start)
dataframe = google_sheet.get_content_as_pd_dataframe(worksheet))
print(dataframe.head())
```
The output looks like this: 

>   Store code Lenght of interview(minutes)  
> 0   Sto_code                     leng_int 
> 1       3082                           34      
> 2       1718                           18      
> 3       1434                           24      
> 4        739                           18      

So it seems that the code is not takin into account the start parameter. 
I found the workaround:
```
    new_header = dataframe.iloc[0]  # grab the first row for the header
    dataframe = dataframe[1:]  # take the data less the header row
    dataframe.columns = new_header  # set the header row as the df header
```

But I would like you to know about the issue.

issue_title: Accept verbosity option while creating Django backend
issue_body:
`PostmarkClient` accepts a `verbosity` option. However, there is no way to specify this option when using the Django backend. My workaround is to add a `VERBOSITY` field in `settings.POSTMARK` and use this field's value in my custom backend. It would be good to have this functionality in `postmarker`.

```python
from postmarker.core import PostmarkClient
from postmarker.django import EmailBackend as PostmarkerDjangoEmailBackend

class EmailBackend(PostmarkerDjangoEmailBackend):
    def open(self):
        if self.client is None:
            verbosity = self.get_option('VERBOSITY') or 0
            self.client = PostmarkClient(token=self.token, verbosity=verbosity)
        return False
```

issue_title: stPipe not called when table is inside a directive
issue_body:
Hi, 

We have a case where the pipe function that makes a HTTP call and gets a page is dependent on a set of inputs. The pipe function doesn't make sense to be executed till those inputs are provided. 

How we've managed to do this so far is to have a `load` variable on the scope which is checked in the pipe function. If `load` isn't set to `true`, the pipe function doesn't proceed. The `load` variable is set to true only when all inputs required for the page to be brought from the backend are on the scope. Following this we manually call the pipe function. The reason we are able to do this and manually fire the pipe function again is because on the initial call of the pipe function, we store `tableState` on the scope and pass that in when we make the first call.

Now, we are having a need to abstract this very same component into the directive. The expectation was that it would work straight away but then I noticed that the pipe function **isn't** called when the table was placed inside a directive. I've set up a [Plunker](http://plnkr.co/edit/rMsAfXWzQG17sTMGgB7H?p=preview) to demonstrate this. 

I might be _completely_ wrong in my approach and if you could suggest a better / the right way to go about this, I am all for trying it. 

Additionally do let me know if you need any more information from my side. I will update the plunker as per your requests.

Thanks again for open sourcing this great library. I understand that it will take up a lot of your time and resources. Cheers :beers:

Thank you again for this lib.

Regarding the management of this project, I would like to make 2 suggestions:
- Would it be possible to use tagged-releases and/or milestones to track fixed bugs?
For instance, I'm trying to see if #160 is included in latest PyPi release.
- Usually the documentation-link (https://pypi.python.org/pypi/exchangelib) of a project is included at the top of the GitHub-repo (just click the `Edit` button to the top of the landing-page and set it).
  Even better, you may include all info links using [RST's field-lists](http://docutils.sourceforge.net/docs/ref/rst/restructuredtext.html#field-lists) beneath  `README.rst`'s header-line like this (but need maintenance afterwards):

####################################
Exchange Web Services client library
####################################

:date:          2017-06-22
:home:          https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/
:pypi-repo:     https://pypi.python.org/pypi/exchangelib/

issue_title: Computing P(alive) Using the BG/NBD Model
issue_body:
I found that for all one-time buyers, the P(alive)==1.0, which is rather strange.

And later I find in the paper http://www.brucehardie.com/notes/021/palive_for_BGNBD.pdf that it's the shortcoming of the mathematical formula being used since the model assume that death
occurs after a purchase and that customers are alive at the beginning of the
observation period. The paper also introduced a variant of the basic BG/NBD model which also estimate the number of dead at the beginning of observation period. It would be nice if that variant will be included in the library. I'm not yet able to figure out how to find the parameters of the likelihood function, may spend some time on it but not sure if I can make it since I don't have experience in solving likelihood function.

issue_title: Renaming polymodel migrations in django 1.8
issue_body:
Hello!

i am using django-polymodels and I needed to rename a model which inherits from another model which inherits from PolymorphicModel.  I renamed the model and ran makemigrations which properly detected the rename and made RenameModel migrations. However, when executing this, I got a warning saying that the content type of my old name is stale and needs to be deleted. When answering 'yes' to that, all model instances are deleted by cascading.

As I am not using content types myself, I figured it is related to the models being a polymodel since django-polymodels uses content types. I tried to circumvent this in many ways, for example by creating a new model to copy over all data in a data migration and then deleting the old model, but I also ran into problems with polymodels in the django 1.8 migrations. When loading models like so:

CombinedProduct = apps.get_model("product", "CombinedProduct")

and creating instances like CombinedProduct.create(...) or CombinedProduct(), I get an error saying that content type id null constraint failed. So it seems impossible to create new instances of models based on polymodels through the method advised in migrations.

Are these issues just me, or are they issues in the polymodels library? If not, how do I do proper rename migrations with django-polymodels?

issue_title: Specifying a start or end time of midnight for intraday_time_series causes a TypeError
issue_body:
If you call the `intraday_time_series` method and specify a start time or end time of midnight (i.e. 00:00), a TypeError is thrown. Minimum working example:

```
import fitbit
from datetime import time
from secrets import consumer_key, consumer_secret, user_key, user_secret

client = fitbit.Fitbit(consumer_key, 
                       consumer_secret, 
                       resource_owner_key=user_key, 
                       resource_owner_secret=user_secret)
client.intraday_time_series("activities/steps", start_time=time(0,0), end_time=(23,59))
```

This behaviour is caused by lines 376 and 379 of api.py, i.e.

```
if start_time and not end_time:
    raise TypeError("You must provide an end time when you provide a start time")

if end_time and not start_time:
    raise TypeError("You must provide a start time when you provide an end time")
```

The problem is that if `start_time = time(0,0)`, then it evaluates to `False` as a boolean (the same goes for `end_time`) so the check `if start_time and ...` will always evaluate to `False`. I think the idea here is to check that `start_time` is not equal to `None`, so the fix here should be pretty simple:

```
if start_time is not None and not end_time:
    raise TypeError("You must provide an end time when you provide a start time")

if end_time is not None and not start_time:
    raise TypeError("You must provide a start time when you provide an end time")
```

issue_title: parse_x_forwarded_for uses wrong IP from X-Forwarded-For
issue_body:
Currently Daphne uses the right-most IP when parsing `X-Forwarded-For` while the original client would be the left-most IP.

See: [Wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X-Forwarded-For#Format) and [Twisted implementation](https://github.com/twisted/twisted/blob/e920939343ac5f6795c4509e0fe25c33a2447531/src/twisted/web/http.py#L2361-L2362).

issue_title: How to sync files to various machines when submitting job on Yarn
issue_body:
I can't find any `yarn` example so try myself. But I encounter a problem, namely how to sync files to all machines like that of `ssh` mode.
```
../dmlc-core/tracker/dmlc-submit \
     --cluster yarn \
     --queue root.default \
     --num-workers 2 \
     --num-servers 2 \
     --worker-cores 1 \
     --worker-memory 1g \
     --server-cores 1 \
     --server-memory 1g \
     --ship-libcxx /lib64/ \
     --hdfs-tempdir /tmp \
     --jobname img-classification \
     --archives mxnet.zip \
     python ./mxnet/example/image-classification/train_mnist.py --network lenet --kv-store dist_sync
```

`mxnet.zip` contains
```
python/mxnet
libmxnet.so
example/image-classification
```
I know this is problematic like blow. But I don't find the right way. Thanks
```

    from common import find_mxnet, fit
ImportError: No module named common
```

issue_title: Trying to evaluate MXNet vs TensorFlow but failing to converge a model with same parameters
issue_body:
Hello,
I am trying to evaluate MXNet vs Tensorflow. I Created a model with the same parameters for both frameworks for the mNist dataset.
For some reason I am able to get an evaluation of 90% on the validation dataset when using TensorFlow, but I only get 10% when using MXNet. I believe that I might be missing something when using the MXNet api. Please help me understand what I am doing wrong.

This is the TensorFlow implementation:

``` python
batch_size = 16
patch_size = 5
depth = 16
num_hidden = 64
beta1 = 0.005
beta2 = 0.005
drop_rate=1.0

graph = tf.Graph()

with graph.as_default():

  # Input data.
  tf_train_dataset = tf.placeholder(
    tf.float32, shape=(batch_size, image_size, image_size, num_channels))
  tf_train_labels = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, shape=(batch_size, num_labels))
  tf_valid_dataset = tf.constant(valid_dataset)
  tf_test_dataset = tf.constant(test_dataset)

  # Variables.
  layer1_weights = tf.Variable(tf.truncated_normal(
      [patch_size, patch_size, num_channels, depth], stddev=0.1))
  layer1_biases = tf.Variable(tf.zeros([depth]))
  layer2_weights = tf.Variable(tf.truncated_normal(
      [patch_size, patch_size, depth, depth], stddev=0.1))
  layer2_biases = tf.Variable(tf.constant(1.0, shape=[depth]))
  layer3_weights = tf.Variable(tf.truncated_normal(
      [image_size // 4 * image_size // 4 * depth, num_hidden], stddev=0.1))
  layer3_biases = tf.Variable(tf.constant(1.0, shape=[num_hidden]))
  layer4_weights = tf.Variable(tf.truncated_normal(
      [num_hidden, num_labels], stddev=0.1))
  layer4_biases = tf.Variable(tf.constant(1.0, shape=[num_labels]))
  keep_prob = tf.placeholder(tf.float32)

  # Model.
  def model(data):
    conv = tf.nn.conv2d(data, layer1_weights, [1, 1, 1, 1], padding='SAME')
    hidden = tf.nn.relu(conv + layer1_biases)        
    maxpool = tf.nn.max_pool(hidden, [1, 3, 3, 1], [1, 2, 2, 1], padding='SAME')
    conv = tf.nn.conv2d(maxpool, layer2_weights, [1, 1, 1, 1], padding='SAME')
    hidden = tf.nn.relu(conv + layer2_biases)
    maxpool = tf.nn.max_pool(hidden, [1, 3, 3, 1], [1, 2, 2, 1], padding='SAME')
    shape = maxpool.get_shape().as_list()
    reshape = tf.reshape(maxpool, [shape[0], shape[1] * shape[2] * shape[3]])
    hidden = tf.nn.relu(tf.matmul(reshape, layer3_weights) + layer3_biases)
    model = tf.matmul(hidden, layer4_weights) + layer4_biases 
    return model

  # Training computation.
  logits = model(tf_train_dataset)
  logits = logits + beta1*tf.nn.l2_loss(layer3_weights) + beta2*tf.nn.l2_loss(layer4_weights)
  loss = tf.reduce_mean(
    tf.nn.softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits, tf_train_labels))


  global_step = tf.Variable(0)  # count the number of steps taken.
  learning_rate = tf.train.exponential_decay(0.05, global_step, 900, 0.25)
  # Optimizer.
  optimizer = tf.train.GradientDescentOptimizer(learning_rate).minimize(loss, global_step=global_step)

  # Predictions for the training, validation, and test data.
  train_prediction = tf.nn.softmax(logits)
  valid_prediction = tf.nn.softmax(model(tf_valid_dataset))
  test_prediction = tf.nn.softmax(model(tf_test_dataset))

  num_steps = 1001

  with tf.Session(graph=graph) as session:
    tf.initialize_all_variables().run()
    print('Initialized')
    for step in range(num_steps):
      offset = (step * batch_size) % (train_labels.shape[0] - batch_size)
      batch_data = train_dataset[offset:(offset + batch_size), :, :, :]
      batch_labels = train_labels[offset:(offset + batch_size), :]
      feed_dict = {tf_train_dataset : batch_data, tf_train_labels : batch_labels, keep_prob: drop_rate}
      _, l, predictions = session.run(
        [optimizer, loss, train_prediction], feed_dict=feed_dict)
      if (step % 50 == 0):
        print('Minibatch loss at step %d: %f' % (step, l))
        print('Minibatch accuracy: %.1f%%' % accuracy(predictions, batch_labels))
        print('Validation accuracy: %.1f%%' % accuracy(
          valid_prediction.eval({keep_prob:1.0}), valid_labels))
    print('Test accuracy: %.1f%%' % accuracy(test_prediction.eval(feed_dict={keep_prob:1.0}),   test_labels))
```

This is the MXNet implementation:

``` python
import mxnet as mx
import mxnet.misc
import logging
kv = mx.kvstore.create('local')
head = '%(asctime)-15s Node[' + str(kv.rank) + '] %(message)s'
logger = logging.getLogger()
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG, format=head)
logging.info('startingggg')
logger.setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
logger.info('startingggg')

batch_size = 16
patch_size = 5
depth = 16
num_hidden = 64
beta1 = 0.005
beta2 = 0.005
drop_rate=1.0
num_labels = 10

data = mx.symbol.Variable('data')
loc = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=data, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=depth, name="conv1")
loc = mx.symbol.Activation(data = loc, act_type='relu')
loc = mx.symbol.Pooling(data = loc, pool_type="max", kernel=(3,3),stride=(2,2))
loc = mx.symbol.Convolution(data=loc, kernel=(5,5), num_filter=depth, name="conv2")
loc = mx.symbol.Activation(data = loc, act_type='relu')
loc = mx.symbol.Pooling(data = loc, pool_type="max", kernel=(3,3),stride=(2,2))
loc = mx.symbol.Flatten(data=loc)
loc = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=loc, num_hidden=num_hidden)
loc = mx.symbol.Activation(data = loc, act_type='relu')
loc = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data=loc, num_hidden=num_labels)
loc = mx.symbol.SoftmaxOutput(data=loc, name='softmax')
mxmodel = mx.model.FeedForward(symbol=loc, optimizer=mx.optimizer.SGD(wd=beta1,learning_rate=0.05), 
                     lr_scheduler=mx.misc.FactorScheduler(900,0.25), num_epoch=1)
train_dataset= np.reshape(train_dataset,(200000, 1, 28, 28))
valid_dataset=np.reshape(valid_dataset,(10000, 1, 28, 28))

train_iter = mx.io.NDArrayIter(train_dataset, train_labels, batch_size=batch_size, shuffle=True)
valid_iter = mx.io.NDArrayIter(valid_dataset, valid_labels, batch_size=batch_size)

batch_end_callback = []
batch_end_callback.append(mx.callback.Speedometer(batch_size, 50))

mxmodel.fit(train_iter,eval_data=valid_iter, batch_end_callback = batch_end_callback, eval_metric = ['accuracy'])

issue_title: test_optimizer.test_signum has fixed seed that can mask flakiness
issue_body:
The unit test in title have been using fixed seed to mask flakiness. Suggested action:
1. Evaluate whether the test is flaky without fixed seed. If not, remove seed. Else move to 2
2. If test is flaky, determine whether it's an actual uncaught edge case. If so, fix the operator. Else move to 3
3. If numerical instability is inevitable, adjust tolerance level appropriately.

issue_body:
AjaxSelectWidget displays the value instead of the tile of a item from a SimpleVocabulary. 

To reproduce create a dx-content type wit a field using the AjaxSelectFieldWidget and a SimpleVocabulary. 
```python
DummyVocabulary = SimpleVocabulary(
    [SimpleTerm(value=u'gold', title=_(u'Gold Sponsor')),
     SimpleTerm(value=u'silver', title=_(u'Silver Sponsor'))])
```

Use it in a schema:

```python
dummyfield = schema.List(
    title=_(u'Dummy'),
    value_type=schema.Choice(
        title=_(u'Dummy'),
        vocabulary=DummyVocabulary),
    required=False,
)
form.widget(
    'dummyfield',

    vocabulary=DummyVocabulary,
)
```

When I edit the field I see the correct options (e.g. "Gold Sponsor"). That is also used when I select a value. But after saving a value and when re-editing the object the saved value displayed in the field will now be "gold" instead of "Gold Sponsor". 

The problem does not appear when using a NamedVocabulary:

issue_title: SSH connection error in Python3
issue_body:
I had problems connecting to a remote ipython cluster, where both endpoints were running Python3. `pyzmq` is used for the connection.

I'm reporting this here, though I'm not sure where the actual bug is.

Traceback: https://gist.github.com/nikitakit/23e92cd94ccde6dfa8e1

My fix is to make `_password_pat` a byte regex instead of a string regex.
```python
import re, zmq
zmq.ssh.tunnel._password_pat = re.compile(b'pass(word|phrase):', re.IGNORECASE)
```

After running this code, the connection is successfully created and appears to function normally.

issue_title: .rsplit() in create_app_revision() not raising IndexError
issue_body:
[via @jdagilliland, @ysaletore]

If a user passes in an id like
`id = name/project/app_id/revision`
then this method works. But since the method is also passed revision, this seems _redundant_ - so the user might try instead
`id = name/project/app_id`

Now the app_id will be split off (instead of the revision) and there seems to be no check that it was a string. In the project, the user will have an app called
`name/project/revision/0`
where revision was passed into create_app_revision()

issue_title: Node Hierarchy and mTransformation accumlation
issue_body:
I don't know this is an issue, probably not, but let me ask anyway.

The sample pseudocode about The Node Hierarchy in [Data Structures page](http://assimp.org/lib_html/data.html) is accumlating mTransformation like
`transform = node.mTransformation * accTransform;`

But [this popular tutorial](http://ogldev.atspace.co.uk/www/tutorial38/tutorial38.html) is accumulating mTransformation like
`GlobalTransformation = ParentTransform * NodeTransformation;`
(Note `ParentTransform` is the accumlated transfrom and `NodeTransformation` is mTransformation of the node if there's no animation)

In practice, the latter works correctly. Is the former just wrong? Am I missing something?

issue_body:
The APS crawler is not scraping references as the JSON API do not seem to provide them:
http://harvest.aps.org/docs/harvest-api#example

So in this case the XML output might be a better source as it seem to have references. See fulltext xml example here: http://harvest.aps.org/docs/harvest-api#retrieve-all-open-access-articles

Since the crawler today works already well with the JSON format, we can adjust it to yield a secondary request per record to the XML endpoint (basically the fulltext with `Accept: text/xml`

issue_title: Submodule.update() not working as expected
issue_body:
I did spend a long time trying to figure out whether this was actually an issue or just a misunderstanding of the documentation. Please let me know if I should be using another method.

I am looping through my main module submodules and checking whether the current head is the reference commit (the one registered on the main module). If it's not, I call `update()` on the submodule. I expected it to have the same effect of running `git submodule update <path>`, but it's not always the case, especially in the situation I'm trying to handle - when someone has changed branches inside the submodule and eventually decides to deploy the main module.

* Let's call my main module M and the submodule I'm handling S.
* The reference commit for S, registered on M, is the latest commit on the `master` branch. 

Initially, I have S updated through command line, currently on reference commit in detached HEAD state, as expected.
If I run, for example
```
$ cd <path_to_S>
$ git status
HEAD detached at 0a3f3c5 # latest commit on master branch
nothing to commit, working directory clean
$ git checkout HEAD~5
Previous HEAD position was 0a3f3c5... Some message'
HEAD is now at 42372c8... Another message.
```

And then run my script in the main module, which is something like:
```
    repo_M = Repo(M_path)
    for sub in repo_M.submodules:
        # Check if submodule is cloned
        try:
            sub_repo = sub.module()
        except exc.InvalidGitRepositoryError:
            [...]
            continue

        # Check if submodule has local changes
        if sub_repo.is_dirty():
            [...]

        # Check if submodule is in reference commit
        if sub.hexsha != sub_repo.head.commit.hexsha:
            print(sub.name + ': currently on commit \'' + str(sub_repo.head.commit.hexsha)
                      + '\'. Checking out reference commit \'' + str(sub.hexsha) + '\'...')
            try:
                sub.update()
                print(sub.name + ': now on commit \'' + str(sub_repo.head.commit.hexsha))

            except:
               [...]
```
It correctly checks out the latest commit in master branch.
However, If I run, instead:
```
$ cd <path_to_S>
$ git status
HEAD detached at 0a3f3c5 # latest commit on master branch
nothing to commit, working directory clean
$ git checkout development
Previous HEAD position was 0a3f3c5... Some message'
Switched to branch 'development'
Your branch is up-to-date with 'origin/development'.
```
And then execute the script on the main module, it does not check out the reference branch. The hexsha printed on the last message is still the same as before, referencing the latest commit on the `development` branch. Verifying from the command line, S is also still on `development`.

I had a look at the code and think it might be due to the bit that checks that it's updating to a newer commit than the current one.

Also, `git submodule update` works if there are untracked files in the submodule, while `sub.update()` raises a `RepositoryDirtyError` exception. I know I could probably handle this some other way, but as above I'm coming from the assumption both should have similar behavior.

Again, I may have misunderstood what the method is for. If that is the case, I appreciate advice on what is the recommended way to check out the reference commit.

issue_title: Unpin mapbox-sdk-py requirement
issue_body: We're developing this project outside of the mapbox-sdk-py project for product reasons, but want to keep it well synchronized. For 0.1, I set `mapbox==0.5.0`, which was a mistake because it prevents CLI users from getting mapbox-sdk-py bug fixes. issue_title: Subscription or workaround?
issue_body:
I have noticed that the Java implementation offers an easy to understand approach to listen to remote events — https://github.com/pusher/pusher-websocket-java#subscribing-to-channels. 

I wonder if this is also existent here. From reading the main page read me, the closest thing to subscription seems to be around the section entitled "Authenticating Channel Subscription". However is really unclear a lot of statements IMHO: 

* "In order for users to subscribe to a private" — which users? 

* "The client will make a POST request to an endpoint (either "/pusher/auth" or any which you specify) with a body consisting of the channel's name and socket_id." — which client? 

I found quite confusing this specific section and the above points plus also the sample: 

```
auth = pusher.authenticate(

  channel=u"private-channel",

  socket_id=u"1234.12"
)
# return `auth` as a response
```

Which does not indicate what to do with such auth.

issue_title: Native consumer hits error backoff delay after topic EOF in end-to-end testing.
issue_body:
Good day!
I have been testing end-to-end performance of pykafka, similar to what is done in the [EndToEndLatency](https://github.com/apache/kafka/blob/trunk/core/src/main/scala/kafka/tools/EndToEndLatency.scala) test.
I have been very surprised that using rdkafka often made the latency considerably higher. Here is my test harness:

```python
from time import time
from random import random
from os import getpid

from pykafka import KafkaClient
from pykafka.common import OffsetType

TIMEOUT = 6000
TOPIC = 'test'
KEY = 'key'

client = KafkaClient(
    zookeeper_hosts='localhost:2181',
    broker_version='0.10.2',
    socket_timeout_ms=TIMEOUT)
topic = client.topics[TOPIC]


def produce_and_consume(key, value, producer, consumer):
    producer.produce(value, key)
    while True:
        t = time()
        msg = consumer.consume()
        delta = time() - t
        if msg.partition_key != key:
            continue
        if msg.value != value:
            continue
        return delta

if __name__ == '__main__':
    num_seen = 0
    pid = getpid()
    producer = topic.get_producer(
        True,
        sync=True,
        retry_backoff_ms=10,
        linger_ms=1)
    producer.start()
    for use_lib in (True, False):
        deltas = []
        consumer = topic.get_simple_consumer(
            "a_group", use_lib,
            auto_commit_enable=False,
            auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.LATEST,
            fetch_wait_max_ms=0,
            # fetch_error_backoff_ms=1,
            consumer_timeout_ms=TIMEOUT)

        for i in range(20):
            value = "%d_%d" % (pid, i)
            delta = produce_and_consume(KEY, value, producer, consumer)
            deltas.append(delta)
        print "lib=%d, n=%d, low=%f, high=%f, avg=%f" % (
            use_lib, i, min(deltas), max(deltas),
            sum(deltas) / len(deltas))
        consumer.commit_offsets()
        consumer.stop()
    producer.stop()
```
Here are typical results:
```
lib=1, n=19, low=0.499292, high=1.004887, avg=0.526834
lib=0, n=19, low=0.000024, high=0.009366, avg=0.001291
```

This was done with kafka 0.10.2, pykafka 2.5.0 or 2.6.0.dev2, on MacOS 10.12.3.

After some analysis, I realized that we were almost always going through the recursive use of Consumer_consume following a `RD_KAFKA_RESP_ERR__PARTITION_EOF` [here](https://github.com/Parsely/pykafka/blob/c450f7ecc800e43fd6faef3dd8fdae20d1e9372d/pykafka/rdkafka/_rd_kafkamodule.c#L985)
(I am not sure why it hits EOF with a synced topic; I tried with native or non-native producers, but it has no impact. On an unrelated note, I wonder why the Topic.get_synced_producer always gives a non-native producer, is that intentional?)

After some more research, I found that this is a known feature of rdkafka: EOF is counted as an error and triggers the error backoff. See
https://github.com/edenhill/librdkafka/issues/1024
(even though we are not in the `enable.partition.eof = false` case, the rest of the discussion is relevant.)

I tried setting a low `fetch.error.backoff.ms` by adding that parameter to pykafka (commented parameter in my harness), and I got perfectly fine results with the rdkafka in that way.
It is a temporary solution, until the librdkafka issue is fixed, but it is the first one I have to propose at this point.

issue_title: internationalize client
cursor behavior for full/ambiguous width characters
issue_body:
Currently I'm using following hack in my program:

```
--- a/pyte/screens.py
+++ b/pyte/screens.py
@@ -28,6 +28,7 @@ from __future__ import (
     absolute_import, unicode_literals, division
 )
 
+import bsdconv
 import copy
 import math
 import operator
@@ -36,6 +37,7 @@ from itertools import islice, repeat
 
 from . import modes as mo, graphics as g, charsets as cs
 
+width_counter=bsdconv.Bsdconv("utf-8:width:null")
 
 try:
     xrange
@@ -392,6 +394,10 @@ class Screen(list):
         char = char.translate([self.g0_charset,
                                self.g1_charset][self.charset])
 
+       width_counter.conv(char.encode("utf-8"))
+       width_info=width_counter.info()
+       width=width_info['full']*2+width_info['ambi']*2+width_info['half']
+
         # If this was the last column in a line and auto wrap mode is
         # enabled, move the cursor to the next line. Otherwise replace
         # characters already displayed with newly entered.
@@ -410,9 +416,13 @@ class Screen(list):
         self[self.cursor.y][self.cursor.x] = self.cursor.attrs \
             ._replace(data=char)
 
+       if width>1:
+               self[self.cursor.y][self.cursor.x+1] = self.cursor.attrs \
+                   ._replace(data="")
+
         # .. note:: We can't use :meth:`cursor_forward()`, because that
         #           way, we'll never know when to linefeed.
-        self.cursor.x += 1
+        self.cursor.x += width
 
     def carriage_return(self):
         """Move the cursor to the beginning of the current line."""
```

there should be a similar but cleaner way to determine character width, and especially,  there is a set of "east-asian ambiguous width" characters, its width depends on locale, I treat it as fullwidth (*2) in my case.

http://www.unicode.org/Public/UNIDATA/EastAsianWidth.txt
https://github.com/buganini/bsdconv/blob/master/codecs/inter/WIDTH.c

Aliasing for line_aa with slope greater than 1
issue_body:
skimage.draw.line_aa() aliases when the slope is greater than 1 in magnitude. It works correctly, however, when the slope is less than 1 in magnitude.

Below is a unit test. When the bug is fixed, the final assertion should succeed. Also, once the bug is fixed, you should be able to see that the two printed arrays are near transposes of each other.

Test code:

    from skimage.draw import line_aa
    import numpy

    img1 = numpy.zeros((10, 10), dtype=numpy.uint8)
    rr, cc, val = line_aa(4, 1, 5, 8)       # Draw almost horizontal line
    img1[rr, cc] = val * 255
    print 'Correct:'
    print img1

    img2 = numpy.zeros((10, 10), dtype=numpy.uint8)
    rr, cc, val = line_aa(1, 4, 8, 5)       # Draw almost vertical line
    img2[rr, cc] = val * 255
    print 'Incorrect (aliased):'
    print img2

    # Take difference between transposed img2 and img1
    diff = (numpy.array(img1, float)-numpy.array(img2.transpose(),float))
    print 'Difference between the two after transposition:'
    print diff
    diff_ssd = numpy.sqrt(numpy.sum(diff**2))
    assert diff_ssd < 50, 'Expected difference to be small'

issue_title: Adding a user to the superuser-access role locks out from viewing all unregistered users
issue_body:
Minimal reproduction: https://github.com/jacquerie/invenio-records-ui/blob/40ecddfe45c1a5fc17b87fdb73be8c060912580d/examples/permsapp.py
When visiting `/records/1` as an unregistered user one gets a login page, rather than the record.

I understand that this is expected behaviour given the code, but I want to argue that is not reasonable behaviour given the objective of the library.

Locking out from viewing all unregistered users when a superadmin exists is reasonable behaviour when we are talking about files on a machine: if a `sudo` user exists, by default all files should LOCK access by guests.

This is not reasonable when we are talking about a web server: the mere fact that an admin exists should NOT LOCK access by guests.

Applications built using Invenio are more like web servers, so by default should have the latter behaviour.

CC: @jmartinm @david-caro @kaplun

issue_title: Is python-fire not compatible with numpy??
issue_body:
Here is a simple test

```
import numpy as np
import math
import fire

def test(x):
	a=np.array(x)
	return a

def test2(x):
	a=math.sin(x)
	return a

print('direct call')
print(test([1,2]))

print('fire call')
if __name__ == '__main__':
  fire.Fire()
```

save this as test_fire.py

if we run 

`python test_fire.py test2 1`

it has no problem, it outputs

> [1 2]
> direct call
> [1 2]
> fire call
> 0.8414709848078965

print "ERRORS in geopy.distance.ELLIPSOIDS (distance in km)"

issue_title: Logging from celery worker processes doesn't work under some circumstances
issue_body:
This refers only to the **Threaded transport**.

I use Celery and Raven in my Django project. Project uses some third party libraries that are somewhat outdated and output warnings about some deprecations on initialization.

Also I have enabled WARNING level for sentry log hander.

This results in following failure scenario:

1. Celery worker starts (`celery worker -A myproject')
2. Django initializes models and other stuff
3. Anything is being send into raven (in my case its deprecation warning, but it can be something more important)
4. Raven initializes threaded transport, which starts its thread.
5. ....
6. Celery forks worker processes
7. Worker processes adopt everything from parent celery worker, except threads.
8. Raven in worker process tries to send something to its thread, but the thread doesn't exist and message stays in the queue.

I have found a workaround for this: attach a handler to worker_init Celery's signal and reinitialize all transports there:

```py
from celery.signals import worker_process_init

@worker_process_init.connect
def reset_raven_transport_registry(sender, **kwargs):
    from raven.base import Client, TransportRegistry, default_transports
    Client._registry = TransportRegistry(transports=default_transports)
```

I don't know whose issue is it and I don't know would you fix it or not, I just want to add a solution if someone will encounter this problem, this will save a couple hours of debugging.

issue_title: user_context is overwritten when Sentry(app) called after @before_request
issue_body:
If you initialize `raven.contrib.flask.Sentry` after a Flask `before_request` has been defined (in which `sentry.user_context` is used), the user context is destroyed when sending errors.

For instance, in the code below the user context sent to Sentry only has an ip address.  But if you uncomment "Option A" and comment out the "Option B", the user context contains `email=bob@bob.com` (and the other items).

```python
from flask import Flask
from raven.contrib.flask import Sentry

app = Flask(__name__)

# Option A
# sentry = Sentry(app)
# message = 'Option A'

@app.before_request
def before_request():
    sentry.user_context({
        'ip_address': '1.2.3.4',
        'email': 'bob@bob.com',
        'username': 'someguy',
        'id': '1238923',
    })
    sentry.extra_context({
        'goo': 'bar',
        'ip_address': '1.2.3.4',
        'email': 'bob@bob.com',
        'username': 'someguy',
        'id': '1238923',
    })

# Option B
sentry = Sentry(app)
message = 'Option B'

@app.route('/')
def index():
    raise Exception(message)

app.run(debug=True)
```

I think it's because [`get_user_info` in this spot](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/flask.py#L224) returns `None` and merging `None` overwrites existing values:

```
>>> from raven.context import Context
>>> c = Context()
>>> c.merge({'user': {'foo': 'bar'}})
>>> c
<Context: {'user': {'foo': 'bar'}}>
>>> c.merge({'user': None})
>>> c
<Context: {'user': None}>
```

So maybe the fix is to have `Context.merge` treat `None`s as `{}`.  Or maybe `get_user_info` should return `{}` instead of `None`.

Or maybe this:

```diff
diff --git a/raven/contrib/flask.py b/raven/contrib/flask.py
index a973344..6014c2c 100644
--- a/raven/contrib/flask.py
+++ b/raven/contrib/flask.py
@@ -221,7 +221,7 @@ class Sentry(object):
         except Exception as e:
             self.client.logger.exception(to_unicode(e))
         try:
-            self.client.user_context(self.get_user_info(request))
+            self.client.user_context(self.get_user_info(request) or {})
         except Exception as e:
             self.client.logger.exception(to_unicode(e))
 
```

issue_title: Add flake linting to CI process?
Yet Another Organization Discussion
issue_body:
Hello all,

once more I would like to propose a couple of stuff for the organization of NeuroM in a more intuitive way. I will just write down my ideas and we can discuss it over time. I would appreciate it if we can have a fruitful discussion upon these ideas.

First of all, I think it would be easier and tidier if we had a dedicated module for features where we can develop the latter without having to modify the neuron/population ezy objects. Regardless of the design of the model I think it would be better to have the minimum possible amount of code inside the core objects --the way it is right now-- and then remove entirely the ezy module. This way it will be clear for the user what are the main structures in the software and what are they for.

The connection between that module and the neuron/population objects can vary. It can be in an object oriented way, i.e. Neuron.features which set as an attribute upon initialization:

```python
class Neuron:
    def __init__(self,...):
        self.features = NeuronFeatures(self)

segment_lengths = nrn.features.segment_lengths(*args, **kwargs)
```
 in semi-functional way (maybe it is considered object-oriented, dunno):
```python
fs = NeuronFeatures(nrn)
fs.section_lengths(*args, **kwargs)
```
which can be alternatively implemented in a more general way and create feature objects regardless of the abstraction level through a factory class:
```python
fs = Features(object_with_features)
```
or in a completely functional way:
```python
fs = features(object_with_features, Features.segment_lengths)
```
It is important to stress here that in the last block of code in the features function one will not pass the function itself i.e. segments.foo, but an Enum for the feature. This is mainly the interface for the users in order to be able to see all the available features in a consistent way and not have to search for the specific function in the low or high levels of the code to extract what they need. 

What do you think?

issue_title: list函数同时使用limit和delimiter的逻辑有问题
issue_body:
我要模拟目录的实现,期望的是能够返回指定前缀作为父目录的直属子目录和文件.
前缀为GYyMYR/images/,分隔符为"/"的应该是GYyMYR/images/20160407/的形式,也就是按日期归类的.
因此下面的 list 操作不会有实际文件的key.

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=100, delimiter="/")
可以列出我的四个目录(这是正常的):
GYyMYR/images/20160407/
GYyMYR/images/20160411/
GYyMYR/images/20160807/
GYyMYR/images/20160812/

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=1, delimiter="/")
可以列出我的1个目录(这是正常的):
GYyMYR/images/20160407/

bucket.list(bucket_name, prefix="GYyMYR/images/", marker=marker, limit=2, delimiter="/")
只列出我的1个目录(**出问题的**):
GYyMYR/images/20160407/
想要显示两个的话,limit是25才行
显示3个,limit需要是43

分析结果是,七牛的逻辑处理有问题,limit之所以不好用是因为在查询的时候把过滤掉的文件数量也计算在内了,比如说,limit为1的时候正常是因为第一个文件的前缀就符合,而limit为25的时候才能显示2个目录是因为第一个目录中包含了24个文件,过滤的时候已经计数了,这就是问题的所在.

limit应该限制返回的key数量,而不该包含要过率掉的文件数量

issue_title: reinit command doesn't take into account the node name
issue_body:
Hi,

I've noticed that `patronictl reinit` command does not honor cluster member name and simply bootstraps itself if the role is secondary.

Steps to reproduce the issue:
1) Set up 3 nodes cluster.
2) Log in to the 1st secondary node and try to reinitialize the 2nd one.
```
postgres@node-01:~$ date
Tue Feb 13 09:31:26 UTC 2018

postgres@node-01:~$ patronictl -c /etc/patroni/node-01.yml reinit main
+---------+---------+---------------+--------+---------+-----------+
| Cluster |  Member |      Host     |  Role  |  State  | Lag in MB |
+---------+---------+---------------+--------+---------+-----------+
|   main  | node-01 | 192.168.33.10 |        | running |       0.0 |
|   main  | node-02 | 192.168.33.11 |        | running |       0.0 |
|   main  | node-03 | 192.168.33.12 | Leader | running |       0.0 |
+---------+---------+---------------+--------+---------+-----------+
Which member do you want to reinitialize [node-02, node-03, node-01]? []: node-02
Are you sure you want to reinitialize members node-02? [y/N]: y
Success: reinitialize for member node-02
```
3) Check out log files locally.
```
Feb 13 09:31:34 node-01 patroni[6147]: 2018-02-13 09:31:34.813 UTC [6711] LOG:  database system is shut down
Feb 13 09:31:34 node-01 patroni[6147]: 2018-02-13 09:31:34,817 INFO: Removing data directory: /var/lib/postgresql/10/main
Feb 13 09:31:35 node-01 patroni[6147]: 2018-02-13 09:31:35,418 INFO: replica has been created using basebackup
Feb 13 09:31:35 node-01 patroni[6147]: 2018-02-13 09:31:35,419 INFO: bootstrapped from leader 'node-03'
Feb 13 09:31:35 node-01 patroni[6147]: 2018-02-13 09:31:35,422 INFO: closed patroni connection to the postgresql cluster
```
4) Check out files creation time and server start time.
```
postgres@node-01:~$ psql -c "select access, modification, change from pg_stat_file('PG_VERSION');"
         access         |      modification      |         change         
------------------------+------------------------+------------------------
 2018-02-13 09:31:35+00 | 2018-02-13 09:31:35+00 | 2018-02-13 09:31:35+00
(1 row)

postgres@node-01:~$ psql -c "select pg_postmaster_start_time()"
   pg_postmaster_start_time    
-------------------------------
 2018-02-13 09:31:35.485135+00
(1 row)
```

There's nothing special in log files on the target 'node-02' regarding its re-initialization.

Is this expected, or is it a bug?

issue_title: search method should support start_index and max_results
issue_body:
# Issue
CROWD API search results that exceed the server's maximum result set size are not accessible through the python-crowd.py project. The current search method supports CROWD API query parameter of entity_type for the /usermanagement/1/search resource, but has not implemented support for start_index or max_results parameters. 

# Solution
I propose to enhance the search method to implement two additional query parameters which the CROWD API supports: start_index and max_results. Implementation should include default values of start_index=0 and max_results=99999 to maintain backwards compatibility with existing tools not currently supplying these arguments when calling the search method.

# Benefits
Supporting start_index and max_results query parameters will…

- Allow retrieval of results exceeding the CROWD server's maximum results set size.
- Allow performance tuning if multiple, smaller query results would be better for server or client performance.

# Potential Issues
Existing tools using the python-crowd module that rely upon a server's default max_results setting may encounter a change in behavior as the query results could be larger (or smaller) than anticipated due to the proposed default value of 99999. According to the Atlassian REST API policy, the default and maximum sizes of paged data are not considered part of the API and may change without notice. Tools using the CROWD API should declare and validate results set sizes themselves. [https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging](https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging)

issue_title: BigQuery should have a module that follows the DB-API to allow for a SQLAlchemy dialect
issue_body:
There is a module to connect [SQLAlchemy to BigQuery, but it only has partial support](https://github.com/cpdean/sqlalchemy-bigquery/issues/6). I'd love to be able to connect to BigQuery using SQLAlchemy this way and be able to play with [Caravel](https://github.com/airbnb/caravel) as was mentioned in that partial support issue.

To do this properly, we'd need to provide a module with an [implementation of a SQLAlchemy dialect](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/internals.html#sqlalchemy.engine.default.DefaultDialect) I would imagine we'd want to package this separately so as not to add a dependency on SQLAlchemy. Looking more closely at the [dialect interface](http://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/latest/core/internals.html#sqlalchemy.engine.interfaces.Dialect) it looks like the database driver would need to implement the [Python DB-API](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0249/).

I'm considering this a low priority feature request, but do think it would be cool.

issue_title: How asynchronous should Firestore API be?
issue_body:
This is very much related to @jonparrott's GAX/GAPIC overhaul.

> APIs that involve network operations should follow the language’s common pattern for async requests. Synchronous wrappers can be provided where applicable.

Most methods return a JavaScript Promise, and we could use a `Future` as return values. However, the current implementation is fully synchronous.

----

From @jonparrott 

gRPC supports non-blocking calls. I'm not sure if gax exposes those? If it doesn't it shouldn't be too horribly difficult to do so.

```python
feature_future = stub.GetFeature.future(point)
feature = feature_future.result()
```

issue_title: cloudpickle breaks dill deserialization across servers.
issue_body:
Following up on this issue @mmckerns responded to on Stackoverflow:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/42960637/python-3-5-dill-pickling-unpickling-on-different-servers-keyerror-classtype/43006034#43006034

In a nutshell, with Python 3.5:

**Server A** imports `cloudpickle` this causes `types.ClassType` to become defined.
```
>>> import types
>>> dir(types)
  ['BuiltinFunctionType',
   'BuiltinMethodType',
   'ClassType',
   'CodeType',
   ...
  ]
```
**Server B** does not import `cloudpickle`, so `types.ClassType` is left undefined.
```
>>> import types
>>> dir(types)
  ['BuiltinFunctionType',
   'BuiltinMethodType',
   'CodeType',
   ...
  ]
```
Objects which are serialized in **server A** also seem to serialize a reference to `ClassType`. Then, when they are deserialized on **server B*, we encounter the following error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
 File "/home/streamsadmin/git/streamsx.topology/test/python/topology/deleteme2.py", line 40, in <module>
   a = dill.loads(base64.b64decode(a.encode()))
 File "/home/streamsadmin/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 277, in loads
   return load(file)
 File "/home/streamsadmin/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 266, in load
   obj = pik.load()
 File "/home/streamsadmin/anaconda3/lib/python3.5/site-packages/dill/dill.py", line 524, in _load_type
   return _reverse_typemap[name]
KeyError: 'ClassType'
```

This is because `_reverse_typemap` is populated partly by the contents of `types`, which doesn't define the `ClassType` type by default.

The workaround on __server B__ is to define `ClassType` in `_reverse_typemap` after dill is imported, and before an object is first deserialized.

```
dill.dill._reverse_typemap['ClassType'] = type
```

As a long term workaround, maybe create a whitelist of valid 3.5 types found in the `types` module? A whitelist would eliminate this kind of error and prevent any pollution/side effects from other modules like `cloudpickle`.

issue_title: Exception while parsing gct files
issue_body:
Hi, I wanted to parse a sample gct file but there is a bug in the source code which causes an exception while parsing the file. The function `read_version_and_dims()` in parse_gct.py, there is a problem in its implementation. It opens the file in binary mode, so the 
`version = f.readline().strip().lstrip("#")`
 will make error.
The exception is :
TypeError: a bytes-like object is required, not 'str' when writing to a file

issue_title: acquire_token not thread-safe
issue_body:
The acquire_token method is not thread-safe.

Example:
```
context = adal.AuthenticationContext(authority, api_version=None)

context.acquire_token_with_username_password(resource, username, password, client_id)

# Then executing the method below in parallell when the token cache expires, say with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor will cause a KeyError. 

context.acquire_token(resource, username, client_id)

# The issue seems to be a dict.pop operation during cache update which itself is not thread-safe.
```

issue_title: Using Environment Variables with Lambda
issue_body:
Today I tried to add **Environment Variables** to a Lambda function but this resulted in some issues. Apparently there are 2 PR merged in the 1.9.1 release ( #616 and #631 ). One uses a _dict_, the other uses a _Class_ (which is currently used).

When I try to use this  in a stack where I also use Elastic Beanstalk, the following error occurs:

```
AttributeError: Environment object does not support attribute ApplicationName
```

When I import the _Environment_ Class under a different name like so:
```
from troposphere.awslambda import Environment as lambdaenv
````

And then do something like this:
```
lambda_functionname = t.add_resource(Function(
    'lambdaFunction',
    Condition="IsProd",
    FunctionName=Join("-", [Ref(namespace_proj_param), "prod", "FunctionName"]),
    Environment=lambdaenv(
        Variables={'ENV_VAR': 'varvalue'}
    ),
    Code=Code(
````
Then it works.

Not sure if I'm doing something wrong or if I'm missing something though. But might renaming the _Environment_ Class be a solution to this?

issue_title: globs in v2 allows recursive matching, unlike v1
issue_body:
A glob like this
```python
globs('src/**/*.scala')
```

In v1 effectively ignores the `**` and treats it like `'src/*.scala'`. In v2, it is expanded recursively.

issue_title: Python tests should be run in chroots to enforce proper loose file dependency declaration.
issue_body:
#4780 handled the JVM side of this, but as far as I can tell the pytest runner does not actually change CWD into the chroot, so it is still possible to access loose files (as demonstrated by https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/tests/python/pants_test/pants_run_integration_test.py subclasses not actually needing any `files(..)` target dependencies to succeed). Having undeclared dependencies caused master breakage due to cached tests, and so test caching was disabled in travis in #5016.

This ticket covers adding the option to change working directories, and hopefully using it somewhere in pantsbuild/pants CI... the unit test shards seem like a good target, as they don't require tackling the integration test dependencies.

issue_title: Investigate performance bottlenecks in argument parsing
issue_body:
I ran `./pants goals` through `yappi` and found that the three major bottlenecks roughly correspond to three statements in `OptionsInitializer._setup_options`.  Look for the comment lines with `###` which have timings for the following statement:

```python

  def _setup_options(self, options_bootstrapper, working_set):
    bootstrap_options = options_bootstrapper.get_bootstrap_options()
    global_bootstrap_options = bootstrap_options.for_global_scope()

    # The pants_version may be set in pants.ini for bootstrapping, so we make sure the user actually
    # requested the version on the command line before deciding to print the version and exit.
    if global_bootstrap_options.is_flagged('pants_version'):
      print(global_bootstrap_options.pants_version)
      self._exiter(0)

    # Get logging setup prior to loading backends so that they can log as needed.
    self._setup_logging(global_bootstrap_options)

    # Add any extra paths to python path (e.g., for loading extra source backends).
    for path in global_bootstrap_options.pythonpath:
      sys.path.append(path)
      pkg_resources.fixup_namespace_packages(path)

    # Load plugins and backends.
    plugins = global_bootstrap_options.plugins
    backend_packages = global_bootstrap_options.backend_packages

    # ### 0.595 s
    build_configuration = load_plugins_and_backends(plugins, working_set, backend_packages)

    # Now that plugins and backends are loaded, we can gather the known scopes.
    known_scope_infos = [GlobalOptionsRegistrar.get_scope_info()]

    # Add scopes for all needed subsystems via a union of all known subsystem sets.
    subsystems = Subsystem.closure(
      GoalRunner.subsystems() | Goal.subsystems() | build_configuration.subsystems()
    )
    for subsystem in subsystems:
      known_scope_infos.append(subsystem.get_scope_info())

    # Add scopes for all tasks in all goals.
    for goal in Goal.all():
      known_scope_infos.extend(filter(None, goal.known_scope_infos()))

    # Now that we have the known scopes we can get the full options.

    # ### 0.619 s
    options = options_bootstrapper.get_full_options(known_scope_infos)

    # ### 1.44 s
    self._register_options(subsystems, options)

    # Make the options values available to all subsystems.
    Subsystem.set_options(options)

    return options, build_configuration
```

The actual timings vary depending on the test environment (or whether or not I have my laptop plugged in), but the relative contributions are roughly the same.  The big bottlenecks are:

* Option registration (i.e. `self._register_options`)
* Loading plugins and backends
* Creating lots of `ArgumentParser`s (one per task in the hierarchy: ~300)

It's important to note that these timings may be different when running other commands since `./pants goals` needs to initialize everything correctly to do its job (I think).

Some things we might be able to do to improve performance:

* Make option registration lazy
* Make `ArgumentParser` registration lazy (this seems to be predicated on making option registration lazy; this has no effect by itself)
* Load plugins and backends lazily

I'm still not sure which of the above things pants already does lazily, though.  I haven't studied it carefully enough to tell, yet.

issue_title: Python interpreter not being found depending on location of project
issue_body:
Hi, I've been experimenting with pants with a python library and have set up a very simple example project:

`pants.ini`:
```
[GLOBAL]

backend_packages: [
    'pants.backend.codegen.thrift.python',
    'pants.backend.graph_info',
    'pants.backend.python'
 ]
```
`hello/BUILD`:
```
python_library(
  name='hello',
  sources=globs('*.py'),
  compatibility=['>3.5'],
)
```
`hello/hello.py`:
```
def hello():
    print('hello')
```
`hello/__init__.py`: empty

My system has both a 2.7 interpreter and a 3.5.2 interpreter on the path, so I'd expect pants to use the 3.5.2 interpreter for this library. This works as expected when this directory is in my home directory.

However, if I root this project on an NFS share, and try to build/run, it only finds my 2.7 interpreter. Same code/files, same command run, it just complains about not finding a compatible interpreter.

Should the location of the root have an impact on where pants looks for interpreters? Both interpreters are on my PATH, both in /usr/bin. Any tips for troubleshooting?

issue_title: No tests to verify large downloads work.

issue_title: Should close the iostream (self.content)
issue_body: In GetContentFile and GetContentString, after the content is read, the iostream needs to closed; otherwise, it could lead to serious memory leak.
